text: Laparoscopic repairs supplemented with perineal approach for perineal hernia after abdominal perineal resection are video big net.
This is a case of an eighty-year-old woman with low BMI who underwent laparoscopic APR without the resection of extra-arabeta muscles for lower rectal cancer.
The pathological diagnosis was stage IIIc The patient received adjuvant chemotherapy.
One year later, she underwent hysterectomy for pyometola.

Three years after the resection, the patient complained of perineal bulge and effusion. The CT scan shows protrusion of the small ball through the pelvic floor into the perineal region. A perineal hernia was diagnosed.
The hernia orifice measured six point five centimeter and five point five centimeter on CT scan.
The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position for laparoscopic perineal hernia repair, supplemented by a perineal approach.
The laparoscopic procedure was performed using the five-part method. The part placement is shown in the schema.
The laparoscopic procedure involves creating a new peritoneum, with visual focus on the surgical technique used to form this anatomical layer.
The surgeon observes severe adhesions in the pelvis and proceeds to dissect the adhesions between the intestine and pelvic wall using monopolar electrocautery and scissors.
The surgeon lifts the vagina by suturing to secure a better view of the pelvis, taking care to avoid damaging the adjacent small intestine.
The surgeon moves the intestine to the cranial side, exposing the hernia orifice. The hernia orifice is measured intraoperatively to be seven centimeters by seven centimeters.
The surgeon marks a three-centimeter margin circumferentially around the exposed hernia orifice, preparing for further dissection or repair.

The surgeon shapes the ventral right ST mesh and inserts it into the abdomen through a port in the right lower quadrant. The mesh is then fixed laterally and posteriorly using absorbable tacks, securing it in place.
The surgeon visually confirms the patency of the iliac arteries and veins, ensuring these vascular structures are unobstructed within the surgical field.
The surgeon sutures the mesh at the anterolateral side of the hernia repair site, taking care to avoid damage to the nearby ureter.
The surgeon expands the mesh to cover the pelvic floor, completing the laparoscopic hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the mesh fixation method using a lightweight monofilament polypropylene mesh, which has been trimmed to a three-centimeter circumferential margin around the hernia orifice.

The surgeon fixes the anterior side of the mesh to the posterior wall of the vagina, the posterior side to the regenerated membrane, and the lateral side to the pelvic floor muscles. Absorbable tacks are used postelaterally after confirming the patency of the internal ureac artery and vein, while absorbable sutures are applied antelaterally due to the absence of the visible ureter.
The surgeon follows a perineal approach after completing the laparoscopic procedure, making a skin incision at the site of the perineal hernia and opening the herniated sac.

The surgeon places artisanal sutures circumferentially around the perineal side of the mesh under direct vision. A drone is inserted through the perineal side beneath the mesh. The perineal sac and skin are trimmed, and the perineal side is closed.

The total operative time was two hundred forty-six minutes and blood loss was two hundred cc. There were no post-operative complications. As a four-month follow-up, there was no recurrence at the perineal site.
In conclusion, this technique is feasible and safe and may provide promising surgical options for the repair of post-operative perineal hernias.
